coredata.dc:title: Kalman filter method with correction of open-circuit voltage curve for estimating SOC of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University.
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Xiangdong
Huang, Yanqi
Wu, Xiaomei
coredata.dc:description: This study presents a method for accurately estimating the state-of-charge (SOC) of lithium-ion batteries. By leveraging the correlation between open-circuit voltage (OCV) curves of batteries, the method generalized a customized OCV curve for the battery under test, enhancing the accuracy of SOC estimation. The equivalent circuit model parameters of the battery were identified using a particle swarm optimization with a genetic algorithm (GA-PSO). The SOC was then estimated using a cubature Kalman filter. Experimental results demonstrate that the method achieves a mean absolute error of less than 0.70% and a maximum error of less than 6.45%. The method is applicable to various types of lithium-ion batteries, temperature conditions and working conditions. Furthermore, this study shows that the method significantly improves the accuracy at low sampling rates, achieving an approximate 50% improvement in accuracy. Consequently, this method can be effectively integrated into the battery management systems of electronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
SOC
Kalman filter
Open-circuit voltage curve

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Denoise and estimate SOC and OCV value using CKF
Correct OCV curve using proportional method
Generate customized OCV curve of tested battery using proportional method
Results of parameter identification using GA-PSO
Results of SOC estimation using GA-PSO-CKF with correction of OCV reference curve
The proposed method could correct the results of ampere-hour integral method
The method has advantages compared with other studies

Initialize covariance matrix P, system noise variance matrix Q and observation noise variance R, where Q is the error matrix of the recursive process of the system state equation, and the error comes from the ampere hour integration method and current measurement error, R is the measurement error of the output voltage, and the covariance matrix P is initialized to a small value. In this paper, it is initialized as the covariance matrix of the ampere hour integration method error and the current measurement error.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The impact of parameter identification methods on the estimation results of SOC value.

row.entry: GA-PSO-CKF (Use the mean of two OCV reference curves)

head.ce:title: Kalman filter method with correction of open-circuit voltage curve for estimating SOC of lithium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a method for accurately estimating the state-of-charge (SOC) of lithium-ion batteries. By leveraging the correlation between open-circuit voltage (OCV) curves of batteries, the method generalized a customized OCV curve for the battery under test, enhancing the accuracy of SOC estimation. The equivalent circuit model parameters of the battery were identified using a particle swarm optimization with a genetic algorithm (GA-PSO). The SOC was then estimated using a cubature Kalman filter. Experimental results demonstrate that the method achieves a mean absolute error of less than 0.70% and a maximum error of less than 6.45%. The method is applicable to various types of lithium-ion batteries, temperature conditions and working conditions. Furthermore, this study shows that the method significantly improves the accuracy at low sampling rates, achieving an approximate 50% improvement in accuracy. Consequently, this method can be effectively integrated into the battery management systems of electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have become the preferred power source for modern electronic devices owing to their superior energy density, extended cycle life, enhanced safety profile, and environmental compatibility. Precise estimation of the remaining battery capacity, commonly referred to as State-of-Charge (SOC), is fundamental for ensuring the reliability and performance of battery management systems (BMS) across various applications. In electric vehicles (EVs), inaccurate SOC estimation may result in unexpected stranding or battery degradation due to overcharging. Similarly, in medical implantable devices, precise SOC estimation is crucial for timely battery replacement or recharging to ensure patient safety. SOC, defined as the ratio of remaining capacity to nominal capacity at a specific discharge rate, ranges from 1 (fully charged) to 0 (completely discharged). The increasing demand for EVs and portable electronics has driven significant advancements in SOC estimation techniques, which can be broadly classified into five categories: direct measurement methods, equivalent circuit model (ECM)-based approaches, electrochemical model (EchM)-based methods, artificial intelligence (AI)-based techniques, and multi-parameter joint estimation methods [1].

Direct measurement methods, including ampere-hour integration, open-circuit voltage (OCV), and impedance characterization, rely on basic electrical measurements but are susceptible to measurement noise [2,3], with estimation errors reaching up to 12.4 % [4]. ECM-based methods [5] employ parameter estimation techniques such as recursive least squares (RLS) [6] and particle swarm optimization (PSO) [7] to construct battery models, followed by advanced filtering algorithms like Kalman filters (KF) and particle filters (PF) [8] for noise suppression and accurate SOC estimation. These methods offer a balance between computational efficiency and estimation accuracy, making them widely applicable in practical BMS implementations [9–13]. For example, Wang [14] employed PSO to optimize the computational window of the adaptive square root cubature Kalman filter (ASRCKF), enhancing Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) performance. He also designed a finite-memory RLS algorithm for parameter identification and an adaptive radial basis function correction-differential support vector machine model for SOC correction [15]. Peng [16] used a fuzzy-proportional integral regulator to establish an improved ECM for parallel battery packs and estimated SOC using an adaptive unscented Kalman filter (UKF).

EchM-based methods [17] provide a more fundamental understanding of battery behavior by modeling ion concentration distribution and transfer processes. For example, Gao [18] used a reduced-order electrochemical model and dual nonlinear filters for reliable co-estimation of cell SOC and state-of-health (SOH). While offering higher accuracy in predicting battery dynamics, these methods are computationally intensive and less commonly implemented in real-time systems. AI-based approaches [19–21], particularly those utilizing neural networks, establish complex relationships between battery parameters and SOC through extensive offline training. Recent developments have seen the integration of memory-capable networks like Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) with multi-parameter estimation to account for temporal changes and environmental factors. In recent research, Bao [22] introduced a dual-task GRU framework for joint SOC and state-of-energy (SOE) estimation of lithium-ion battery packs, achieving SOC and SOE errors below 3 % on real vehicle data. Chen [23] constructed a CNN-LSTM framework to extract aging features, correct capacity variations, and estimate SOC precisely.

Among these methodologies, ECM-based approaches have gained prominence due to their favorable trade-off between computational complexity and estimation accuracy. The Thevenin model and second-order RC model are commonly employed [24], with higher-order models offering marginal improvements at the cost of increased computational burden [25]. Parameter identification in ECMs typically employs RLS for its computational efficiency [26] or PSO for its global optimization capabilities. The integration of genetic algorithm (GA) operators into PSO has been shown to enhance parameter estimation accuracy by improving particle diversity [27].

Kalman filter variants, including Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), Adaptive EKF (AEKF) [28], Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) [29], and Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) [30], represent the most widely adopted filtering techniques in SOC estimation. These methods rely on accurate OCV-SOC reference curves, which are typically obtained through discharge experiments. Related studies often assume the OCV reference curve remains accurate when applied to different cells of the same type or across charge–discharge cycles [31]. However, in practice, the OCV-SOC curve varies significantly among batteries of the same type but different individuals, across different cycles of the same battery, and at different ambient temperatures [32]. Despite these variations, most studies simplify the problem by ignoring such differences and directly using the OCV curve as the true value, inevitably introducing errors. Therefore, generating a personalized OCV reference curve for each cell can enhance KF estimation accuracy.

The present study introduces several methodological innovations: (1) development of a personalized OCV curve generation method based on inter-cell correlations within the same battery model; (2) implementation of GA-PSO for ECM parameter identification, employing stable crossover-mutation operations to enhance accuracy while maintaining real-time performance; (3) experimental validation of the proposed method's corrective effect on the ampere-hour integration method under low sampling rates; and (4) comprehensive verification of the method's robustness across different temperatures, operating conditions, and battery types.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 details the mathematical framework and operational procedures of the proposed method, along with experimental data specifications. Section 3 presents comparative analysis of estimation results under various conditions, demonstrating the method's accuracy improvements and temperature compensation capabilities. Section 4 provides in-depth analysis of experimental results, discusses noise sources and suppression mechanisms, and outlines potential enhancements. The paper concludes with a summary of findings and future research directions.

The proposed methodology presents a novel SOC estimation approach that integrates the GA-PSO-CKF algorithm with proportionally corrected OCV reference curves. This method requires prior knowledge of OCV curves
F 2
( S O C )
and
F 3
( S O C )
from two batteries of identical type. The implementation consists of two sequential stages: (1) generation of a customized OCV reference curve through proportional correction, and (2) subsequent SOC estimation utilizing the GA-PSO-CKF algorithm. Fig. 1 illustrates the comprehensive SOC estimation process for lithium-ion batteries, which comprises the following procedural steps:
Data Acquisition: Continuous collection and storage of the tested battery's output current and voltage measurements from the initiation of charge/discharge cycles to the current timestamp.

Customized OCV Curve Generation: Selection of current and voltage data of the tested battery within a specified time window and estimation its Initial SOC using the ampere-hour integration method. Identification of battery parameters (ohmic resistance, polarization resistance, and polarization capacitance) through GA-PSO algorithm based on preliminary SOC values and reference curves
F 2
( S O C )
or
F 3
( S O C )
. Application of CKF for noise reduction and accurate SOC/OCV estimation, generating the test battery's OCV curve
f 1
for the selected period. Derivation of corresponding OCV curves
f 2
and
f 3
for the same period based on
F 2
( S O C )
and
F 3
( S O C )
. Generation of the integrated customized OCV curve
F 1
S O C
for the test battery through proportional correction.

Use the GA-PSO-CKF algorithm to estimate the SOC value at the current and subsequent times: employ the ampere-hour integration method to obtain a preliminary SOC estimate, determine the OCV values based on the personalized OCV curve
F 1
S O C
of the tested battery, identify the battery parameters using the GA-PSO algorithm to establish the ECM of the battery, and use CKF to denoise the preliminary SOC estimations obtained by the ampere-hour integration method to calculate accurate SOC values.

The experimental data utilized in this study were obtained from two primary sources: the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE) [33] and the Mendeley Data repository [34]. The comprehensive database encompasses charge–discharge experimental data for multiple battery types, with each dataset containing detailed information on temporal parameters, charge–discharge current, voltage profiles, and sampling rates. The battery chemistries represented in the database include: LiNixCoMnO2 (INR18650), LiFePO4 (A123), LiCoO2 (CS2), and an additional LiNixCoMnO2 variant (HG2). Table 1 provides a systematic summary of the experimental battery specifications, including maximum voltage thresholds, cut-off voltages, operational temperature ranges, and experimental conditions.

Under low-current charge–discharge conditions, the electrochemical polarization effect is minimal, allowing the output voltage data from constant-current discharge to serve as a reliable reference for the open-circuit voltage (OCV) curve. In contrast, high-current operations, which are more representative of real-world applications, induce significant polarization effects. To simulate practical scenarios, our experiments incorporated two distinct high-current testing protocols: constant-current experiments and dynamic operational condition experiments. The latter involves continuous variations in current magnitude and polarity, effectively simulating battery state transitions in electronic devices, particularly in electric vehicle (EV) applications. The dynamic testing protocols implemented include: Dynamic Stress Test (DST), Highway Driving Schedule (US06), Federal Urban Driving Schedule (FUDS), Beijing Dynamic Stress Test (BJDST), and Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS).

Figs. 2(a) and 2(b) demonstrate the current–voltage characteristics of the INR18650 battery under DST conditions. In Fig. 2(b), the experimental sequence initiates with constant-current discharge until reaching a state of charge (SOC) of 0.5, followed by dynamic charge–discharge cycles under DST protocol. The initial voltage drop during constant-current discharge is attributed to ohmic resistance, while the subsequent voltage recovery reflects the dissipation of ohmic potential. The gradual voltage rebound observed thereafter is indicative of polarization voltage relaxation. Figs. 2(c) through 2(f) present analogous results for two additional battery types under dynamic operational conditions.

The proposed methodology was validated using all four battery types enumerated in Table 1. All computational analyses were performed on a laboratory workstation (OS: Windows 10 64-bit, Memory: 32 GB, CPU: Intel i7-12700 @ 2.10 GHz).

The remaining charge of a lithium-ion battery is expressed by the SOC value. The current
SOC
t
of the battery is the initial value of the battery
SOC
0
plus the value increased or decreased from the beginning to the current time, as shown in Eq. (1):
(1)
SOC
t
=
SOC
0
+
∫
0
t
i ( t )
C
d t
Where
i ( t )
is the output current value of the battery and C is the total capacity of battery.

The ECM used in this paper is Thevenin model. It includes OCV value
U
OC
, ohmic resistance
R o
, polarization internal resistance
R p
, polarization capacitance
C p
and terminal voltage (output battery voltage)
U
, and the voltage of RC circuit is polarization voltage
U p
. The combination of
R p
and
C p
represents the polarization phenomenon generated during charging and discharging.

The polarization phenomenon of lithium-ion battery can be expressed by Eq. (2):
(2)

p , k

.

Estimate the parameters of Eq. (5) to obtain the optimal values of
a
,

and

p , k
by Eq. (7) to establish the ECM of Eq. (2) and (3).
Parameter identification establishes circuit model Eq. (2) and (3), so as to provide noise reduction for subsequent KF filters.

In this paper, the PSO algorithm with GA(GA-PSO) was used for parameter identification. The GA algorithm increases the particle diversity in the PSO algorithm by cross and mutation operation[27], which could enhance the global search ability and robustness of the PSO algorithm.
The steps of GA-PSO algorithm is as follows:

Initialize a group of particles
X | X
i
=
a i
,
b i
,
c i
, i =
1 , 2
⋯ N
, where
N
is the total number of particles, each particle stores different
a , b , c
values. Calculate the fitness
J
of each particle according to Eq. (8). The smaller the fitness value, the better the quality of the particle.
(8)
J = ‖
E k
-
a i
∗
E
k - 1
+
b i
∗
I k
-
c i
∗
I
k - 1
‖

According to Eq. (9), the best two particles with the smallest fitness value are crossed to generate a new particle.
(9)
X
new
= α
X 1
+
( 1 - α )
X 2
Where,
X
new
is the new particle,
X 1
and
X 2
are the best two particles,
α
is the cross coefficient, range from 0 to 1. In this paper,
α
is taken by 0.4.

The traditional mutation operation is carried out by roulette wheel selection which has strong randomness. In this paper, the mutation operation is carried out in the way of Eq. (10). The best two particles are mutated into four new particles with the worst four particles are replaced, so that the calculation amount is not increased on the premise of ensuring the stability of the algorithm [35].
(10)
X
new
=
1 ± β
X n
,
n =
1 , 2
Where,
β
is the mutation coefficient. In this paper,
β
is taken by 0.3,
X 1
and
X 2
are the two particles with the best fitness.

After the cross-mutation operation is performed, calculate the fitness of the new particle, sort the particles by fitness, and take the best particle with the best fitness as the global optimal value
p
gbest
k
at this time.

Then all particles follow Eq. (11) and (12) to change their velocity and position:
(11)
v
i

Where,
k
is the moment,
i
is the particle number,
ω
is the inertia weight,
c 1
is the individual factor,
c 2
is the group factor,
r 1
and
r 2
are the random number between 0 ∼ 1,
v
i
k
is the velocity of the particle at the moment
k

X

th particle storing
a i

value,
p
i , p b e s t
k
is the historical optimal value of the
i
th particle, and
p
gbest
k
is the global optimal value at time
k
.

After the position of particles is updated, calculate the fitness of the particles again, and update the global optimal value and individual historical optimal value. Repeat operations above until all particle values are equal or the maximum number of iterations (100 in this paper) is reached.

After the GA-PSO algorithm outputs the optimal estimate
a , b , c
, the ohmic resistance
R o
, polarization internal resistance
R p
and polarization capacitance
C p
are calculated according to Eq. (7), and the ECM equation is established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Denoise and estimate SOC and OCV value using CKF
ce:section.ce:para:
After establish ECM, system state equation Eq. (13) and observation equation Eq. (14) are composed according to Eq. (1),(2),(3).
(13)
U
p , k
SOC
k
=
e
- T / τ
0
0
1
U
p , k - 1
SOC
k - 1
+
( 1 -
e
- T / τ
)
R
p , k
- T / C
I
k - 1
(14)
U k
=
- 1
U
OC , k
SOC
k
U
p , k
SOC
k
-
I k
R
o , k

The KF could denoise the SOC value obtained by the ampere-hour integration based on Eq. (13) and (14)[9]. EKF and UKF demonstrate strong performance in addressing nonlinear problems. However, the state transition equations of the battery equivalent circuit model can be expressed linearly, as shown in Equations (13) and (14), which diminishes the advantages of using EKF or UKF. CKF solves the problem of high-dimensional divergence because of its dimensional decomposition calculation of the system state matrix, and CKF is more accurate and stable in denoising the lithium-ion battery system [36].
The process of CKF estimating accurate SOC and OCV value is shown in Table 2 :

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correct OCV curve using proportional method
ce:section.ce:para:
In the process of GA-PSO parameter identification and CKF noise reduction, the OCV reference curve is used as lookup table to obtain OCV value
U
OC , k
. But, the OCV curves of different battery individuals are different. Therefore, there are errors with
U
OC
obtained by lookup table in GA-PSO and CKF, and then it results in the errors of battery parameter values and SOC values. At the same time, errors caused by the OCV curve will cause the estimation process to be very unstable. Not only are batteries different from each other, but in the different cycle of charging and discharging of rechargeable batteries, the OCV curve will change in each cycle [37]. And, the OCV curve of the same cell varies at different temperatures [38,39], as shown in Fig. 3(a) .

For non-rechargeable batteries, the battery will not be available after complete discharge, which means that it is impossible to obtain the OCV curve before use. For rechargeable battery, the OCV curve varies in different cycle after it is measured by discharge experiment. Therefore, OCV curves need to be corrected by a method in real time.

The shape of the OCV curve
U
OC
= F
( S O C )
of the same type battery is roughly similar. The correlation coefficient between the OCV curves of the same type battery was calculated, the absolute correlation coefficient value between
F 1
( S O C )
and
F 2
( S O C )
is close to 1, which means that the OCV curves of the same type but different individual batteries have a strong correlation, which exists in all types of batteries tested in this paper.
For example, the correlation coefficient of INR18650 is 1.0000, and the correlation coefficients among the curves at different temperatures in Fig. 3(a) are more than 0.9981.

The necessary and sufficient condition for the correlation coefficient of the two variables to be 1 is that there are constants
l 1
,
l 2
, which satisfy Eq. (15):
(15)
F 1
=
l 1
F 2
+
l 2

In addition, the OCV value at SOC value of 0 is called the cut-off voltage. The material of the same type battery is the same, and its ability to separate and bind electrons is the same, so the cut-off voltage is the same, that is,
F 1
( S O C )
and
F 2
( S O C )
have the same value when SOC is 0. At the same time, the same type of battery will calibrate a cut-off voltage value in the production of batteries. When the voltage drops to this value, it is considered that the battery has been fully discharged.
Thus, the integral OCV curves (SOC from 0 to 1) of the same type but different individual cells meet two conditions: they have a strong correlation and the starting value is the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generate customized OCV curve of tested battery using proportional method
ce:section.ce:para:
Two integral OCV curves
F 2
( S O C )
and
F 3
( S O C )
with cut-off voltage
m
are known.
F 1
( S O C )
is the unknown OCV curve of the battery to be tested. Under the conditions proposed at section 2.5.1, the three curves satisfy Eq. (16):
(16)
F 1
=
l 1
F 2
+
l 2
F 3
=
l 3
F 1
+
l 4
F 3
=
l 5
F 2
+
l 6
F 1
0
= m
F 2
0
= m
F 3
0
= m
Where,
l 1
,
l 2
,
l 3
, l
4
,
l 5
,
l 6
are constants.

According to Eq. (16), it can be deduced as Eq. (17).
(17)
F 3
-
F 1
F 3
-
F 2
=
l 1
l 4
l 6
Where, because the OCV curve is not a constant function,
l 1
could not be 0. Because every curve could not be the same and must meet the same starting value,
l 4
and
l 6
could not be 0. Therefore, there must be a constant
l p
=
l 1
l 4
l 6
that is not 0 and satisfies Eq. (18):
(18)
F 3
-
F 1
F 3
-
F 2
=
l p

Select a range of current and voltage data during a period of time to perform GA-PSO-CKF, and obtain the accurate OCV value changes
f 1
( S O C )
during this period. According to the SOC values, the two reference OCV changes
f 2
S O C
and
f 3
( S O C )
could be obtained by looking up table
F 2
( S O C )
and
F 3
( S O C )
. The three curves
f 1
,
f 2
and
f 3
are shown in Fig. 3(b) (from the INR18650 constant current discharge experiment). Since the three curves are part of three integral OCV curves, Eq. (19) can be obtained by satisfying Eq. (18):
(19)
f 3
-
f 1
f 3
-
f 2
=
l p
Where,
f 1
,
f 2
and
f 3
are known. After
l p
is calculated, the integral customized individual OCV reference curve
F 1
can be generated from Eq. (18). The generated
F 1
is shown in Fig. 3(c).
(20)
F 1
=
l p
F 2
+
( 1 -
l p
)
F 3
Where,
l p
can be regarded as the value that measures which reference curve is closer to
F 1
. Then
F 2
and
F 3
are combined in a certain proportion to generate
F 1
.
After generate and use the customized OCV curve, GA-PSO-CKF could estimate subsequent SOC values more accurately.

In this paper, the mean absolute error (MAE) and maximum error (ME) was used as indicators of SOC estimating accuracy:
(21)
MAE =
1 M
∑
k = 1
M
E r r o r
(22)
ME = M a x
| E r r o r |
Where,
Error
= (actual value – estimated value) / actual value × 100 %,
M
is total number of estimations,
k
is the order of each estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of parameter identification using GA-PSO
ce:section.ce:para: The results of GA-PSO were validated using constant current part of data from INR18650 DST test.

Due to the lack of actual ohmic resistance, polarization resistance and polarization capacitance of the battery in the database, this paper introduces the
U k
and
I k
R
o , k
obtained from parameter identification into the circuit equation Eq. (23). The calculated
U
OC , k
could reflect the results of parameter identification.
(23)
U
OC , k
=
U k
+
U
p , k
+
I k
R
o , k

The results of parameter identification are shown in Fig. 4 . The results using PSO (without GA) are shown in Fig. 4 (a). After performing the cross operation of GA, if the new particles obtained from the cross are added to the particle swarm, the results are shown in Fig. 4 (b). If the new particles obtained from the cross replace the worst particles, the results are shown in Fig. 4 (c). After incorporating the cross and mutation operations of the GA into PSO algorithm, the OCV estimation results obtained from parameter identification are shown in Fig. 4(d). Fig. 4 (e) shows the results which use another OCV reference curve.

Furthermore, the results of parameter identification are used in traditional KF for estimating SOC values (i.e. GA-PSO-KF method for estimating SOC), and the accuracy of the parameter identification are evaluated by accuracy of estimated SOC values. The impact of parameter identification methods on SOC estimation are shown in Table 3 . Without using GA to enhance PSO, the MAE of SOC estimation results reached 16.24 %. After adding cross and mutation operations, the estimation error significantly decreased and the MAE was less than 1 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of SOC estimation using GA-PSO-CKF with correction of OCV reference curve
ce:section.ce:para: The SOC estimation method proposed in this paper was validated using experimental data from multiple batteries.
Fig. 5 shows the comparison of SOC results between traditional KF and CKF, where the red curve represents the estimation results and the blue curve represents the actual value of SOC.

The results of SOC estimation using GA-PSO-CKF on different battery data are shown in Fig. 6 (a) to (d). Using RLS for parameter identification and then using CKF to estimate SOC (RLS-CKF), the estimation results are shown in Fig. 6 (e).

The OCV reference curve used above is the mean of two known OCV reference curves. To illustrate the estimation error caused by the differentiation of OCV reference curves, this paper comparative analysis the results using two different OCV reference curves for SOC estimation. The MAE is 1.07 % using one curve and the other is 0.75 %.

Before using the proportional method to correct the OCV reference curve, it is necessary to verify that the two known OCV reference curves have strong correlation. The correlation coefficient between the reference curves used in this paper is 1.0000 for INR18650 battery, 0.9724 for A123 battery, 0.9999 for CS2 battery and 0.9981 for HG2 battery. The reference curves used in this paper have strong correlation.

Use GA-PSO-CKF to denoise and estimate SOC and OCV value, then use the proportional method to generate a customized OCV reference curve and finally use the customized OCV reference curve to estimate SOC values at all times by GA-PSO-CKF. The SOC estimation results of GA-PSO-CKF with correction of proportional method are shown in Fig. 7 .
The quantitative analysis results of the above methods are shown in Table 4 .

The KF method for estimating SOC is a correction to the results of the ampere-hour integration method. In order to demonstrate the enhancement effect of the proposed method on the results of the ampere-hour integration method, SOC values are estimated by ampere-hour integration method at low sampling rate (1/10 of the original). In order to compare with ampere-hour integration method, estimation results of the method proposed in this paper also perform at the same sampling rate. As shown in Fig. 8 , the blue curve represents the actual value, the green curve represents the ampere-hour integration results at low sampling rates and the red curve represents the results of the method proposed in this paper at low sampling rates. The quantitative analysis results are shown in Table 5 . It can be seen that the method proposed in this paper significantly improves the accuracy of the ampere-hour integration method at low sampling rates.

Due to the correction of the OCV reference curve, the method proposed in this paper is less affected by temperature. This paper tests on various batteries at different temperatures to verify this characteristic. The results are shown in Table 6 (due to the number of HG2-US06 data is too large, data processing was performed after downsampling by 1/10, which results in a decrease in accuracy).

The error from the ECM-based method stems from the following sources: inaccuracies in current measurement and time intervals lead to errors in the ampere-hour integration method; the parameter identification process cannot perfectly estimate model parameters, resulting in loss in estimation results; variations in battery open-circuit voltage curves at different temperatures, among different battery individuals, and under different cycles lead to errors in parameter identification and KF. The method proposed in this paper correct the aforementioned errors through CKF/GA-PSO/ generating personalized OCV curves by proportional method, and possesses multiple advantages. The experimental results demonstrate that the SOC estimating method proposed in this paper is characterized by the following features:
The accuracy and stability of this method are primarily attributed to three key components: parameter identification, CKF-based estimation, and customized OCV curve generation.
(a) GA-PSO for ECM Parameter Identification

The parameter identification results in Fig. 4 demonstrate the effectiveness of the GA-PSO algorithm for ECM parameter identification. While the standalone PSO algorithm exhibits significant fitting errors and curve fluctuations, the integration of cross-operation enhances particle diversity and reduces these fluctuations. Comparative analysis reveals that replacing old particles through cross-operation outperforms the introduction of new particles. The subsequent incorporation of mutation operation effectively eliminates large fluctuations, indicating its substantial contribution to stability enhancement. As evidenced in Table 3, the GA algorithm integration significantly improves SOC estimation accuracy and stability, reducing MAE from 16.24 % to 0.93 % and ME from 39.78 % to 2.99 %. Table 4 further demonstrates that GA-PSO maintains comparable real-time performance with RLS, facilitated by constrained recursive times (<100) and limited particle numbers (20), requiring only minimal computational resources.
(b) CKF for SOC Estimation

Comparative analysis in Table 4 validates the effectiveness of the CKF method. Under identical operating conditions, CKF outperforms traditional KF in both accuracy and stability, achieving approximately 57 % improvement in MAE and 75 % enhancement in ME. The CKF's implementation of state recursion and covariance calculation in two dimensions (
U p
and SOC value) effectively addresses the divergence and accuracy degradation issues encountered by traditional KF in high-dimensional scenarios. This modification significantly enhances both estimation accuracy and algorithmic stability.
(c) Customized OCV Curve Generation

Figs. 4(d) and (e) present a comparative analysis between the GA-PSO identified OCV curve and two reference curves (
f 2
and
f 3
), revealing discrepancies in the tested battery's actual OCV. While existing literature suggests identical OCV curves for batteries of the same model, this assumption introduces estimation errors. Table 4 demonstrates that SOC estimation using the second reference curve yields superior accuracy, attributed to its closer alignment with the tested battery's OCV characteristics. However, determining the most appropriate reference curve in practical applications remains challenging, compounded by the resource-intensive nature of acquiring and storing multiple reference curves.

The proportional method for generating customized OCV curves, coupled with GA-PSO-CKF for SOC estimation, demonstrates enhanced performance in Table 4. This approach reduces MAE to below 0.70 %, indicating improved accuracy and stability, particularly evident in the A123 experiment. The refinement of
U
OC , k
through customized OCV curves enhances the precision of ECM parameters and
U
p , k
identified by GA-PSO, consequently improving CKF's subsequent SOC estimations. Practical implementation suggests that a single OCV curve correction can benefit subsequent SOC estimations.

Equation (15) demonstrates the feasibility of generating customized OCV reference curves with only one known reference curve, requiring estimation of a single unknown parameter through methods such as least squares, hill climbing, or gradient descent. However, CKF's incomplete elimination of
U
OC , k
estimation errors results in a correlation coefficient between
f 1
and
f 2
that fails to approach unity within short timeframes. Consequently, the single-reference curve method exhibits reduced stability and necessitates extensive data collection. While prolonged data collection improves accuracy, it simultaneously increases algorithm runtime. Moreover, significant deviations between the single reference curve and the device's battery characteristics may induce instability in new curve generation. In contrast, the proportional method ensures greater proximity of the second OCV reference curve to the device's battery characteristics, yielding superior performance. Even in cases where neither reference curve closely matches the actual curve, the generation of a new curve through proportional combination of existing curves effectively reduces uncertainty.

In practical application scenarios, the accuracy of the sensors directly impacts the estimation precision. If the voltage and current sensors have excessively low accuracy, the estimation process can become highly unstable. Moreover, inaccurate voltage and current measurements deprive the system of reliable reference values for SOC noise reduction (the principle of Kalman filtering requires accurate real-world information), leading to increased estimation errors. Nevertheless, if the collected voltage and current values are only occasionally inaccurate (e.g., due to fluctuations or noise), the CKF can effectively correct the SOC estimation once accurate measurements are obtained at the next time step. Additionally, communication delay only affects the real-time performance of the output but does not impact the estimation accuracy.

The GA-PSO-CKF algorithm exhibits superior estimation accuracy and broad applicability across diverse operational conditions for multiple battery types. Notably, the A123 battery demonstrates a relatively higher estimation error, with a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 2.85 %, compared to other batteries where the MAE remains below 1 %. This deviation primarily stems from inaccuracies in the discharge experiment data and the lower correlation coefficient of the reference curve. Prior to the application of the customized OCV curve, the estimation error for the A123 battery was substantially larger due to these data inaccuracies. Specifically, the correlation coefficient of the A123 reference curve is 0.9724, the lowest among all tested batteries, whereas the coefficients for other batteries exceed 0.9981. This reduced correlation introduces persistent errors into the customized curve.

In related SOC estimation research, numerous studies have employed diverse methods to account for battery temperature, either by correcting SOC values based on temperature models [40,41] or utilizing neural networks for calibration [42]. These approaches, however, significantly increase algorithmic complexity. In contrast, the proposed method inherently integrates temperature variations into its framework. For Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM)-based methods, temperature primarily affects the OCV reference curve, and the proportional method already incorporates temperature effects into the corrected results. For example, in the A123 battery discharge experiment, to ensure a pronounced difference between the selected reference curves, the OCV reference curves at −10 ℃ and 50 ℃ were chosen, while the ambient temperature of the tested battery was maintained at 20 ℃. Consequently, the customized OCV curve generated by the proportional method accurately corresponds to the OCV curve at 20 ℃ for a specific charge/discharge cycle. Table 5 demonstrates the method's performance under varying temperatures. After correcting the OCV reference curve using the proportional method, temperature variations exert minimal impact on estimation accuracy. Furthermore, compared to results obtained without correcting the OCV reference curve across different temperatures, the accuracy has been significantly enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed method could correct the results of ampere-hour integral method
ce:section.ce:para: The essence of the method based on ECM is to filter the results of the ampere-hour integration method. Therefore, it is contradictory to regard the results of the ampere-hour integration method as the gold standard while believing that the method based on the ECM has improved accuracy. However, most relevant studies have not explicitly explained this issue.

As mentioned in Section 2, the accuracy of the ampere-hour integration method is influenced by factors such as sampling rate, variations in discharge current, and ammeter accuracy during practical applications. The results presented in Fig. 8 and Table 5 demonstrate that when the sampling rate decreases, the error of the ampere-hour integration method increases significantly, with both MAE and ME rising sharply. After applying the method proposed in this paper, the error is reduced, and the result shown in Fig. 8(b) is more evident. Regarding the limitations of the ampere-hour integration method, increasing the sampling rate could mitigate these issues. However, in practical use, increasing the sampling rate also increases the power consumption of the device, which may become a significant burden, particularly for low-power devices where this additional consumption could exceed the energy required for their primary functions. Furthermore, regardless of how much the sampling rate is increased, discrete values cannot be transformed into continuous ones, and errors will still persist. This paper introduces an optimization method specifically designed for low-sampling-rate conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The method has advantages compared with other studies
ce:section.ce:para: The comparative analysis in Table 4 reveals distinct performance characteristics between GA-PSO-CKF and RLS-CKF under DST conditions. While RLS-CKF demonstrates computational efficiency with single-iteration processing, GA-PSO-CKF achieves superior estimation accuracy while maintaining real-time performance. The computational advantage of RLS-CKF is insufficient to offset its reduced accuracy, establishing the GA-PSO algorithm integration as the optimal approach in terms of both precision and efficiency.

Regarding neural network-based estimation methods, LSTM-ACKF, as referenced in [30], achieves a mean square error (MSE, ≈MAE2) of < 2.2 % and a maximum error (ME) below 4 %. Comparatively, the proposed method demonstrates marginally higher maximum error (indicating slightly reduced stability) but achieves better average error performance. Notably, while neural-network-based approaches require extensive experimental datasets for offline training across diverse operating conditions and temperatures, along with retraining for different battery types, our proposed method requires only three discharge curves (two OCV curves and one actual working condition discharge curve). This significantly reduces algorithmic complexity while maintaining excellent real-time performance.

Despite its contributions, this method exhibits several limitations that warrant further investigation. Foremost, the dynamic variation in battery capacity during charge–discharge cycles significantly impacts the accuracy of SOC estimation, necessitating capacity correction mechanisms [43–45]. Furthermore, the method's applicability is constrained by specific conditions, particularly requiring batteries to exhibit a monotonic OCV curve with the established relational characteristics. The experimental validation, while promising, remains limited to specific battery types, indicating a need for broader verification across diverse battery chemistries. Comparative analysis reveals that the method's stability falls short of neural- network based approaches, suggesting room for improvement. Future research directions should focus on two key enhancements: (1) integration of established battery capacity correction methodologies, and (2) synergistic combination with neural network techniques [46] to achieve superior SOC estimation accuracy and robustness.

This study presents an accurate SOC estimation method through innovative OCV curve customization based on intrinsic correlation analysis. The proposed approach significantly enhances the accuracy and stability of both ECM parameters and SOC estimation values. A hybrid GA-PSO optimization algorithm is employed for ECM parameter estimation, achieving both high precision and real-time computation capability (within 9.60E-03 s). Extensive validation across multiple battery types and operating conditions demonstrates the method's superior performance, with Mean Absolute Error (MAE) below 1.66 % across different temperature scenarios, inherently accounting for thermal effects. Comparative analysis with existing methods reveals significant improvements in estimation accuracy, algorithmic efficiency, and real-time performance. Furthermore, the study provides empirical evidence supporting the KF effectiveness in enhancing the accuracy of the ampere-hour integration method, particularly through noise reduction in low-power scenarios. These findings offer a robust solution for battery management systems across various applications.

Xiangdong Cui: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Yanqi Huang: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Xiaomei Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
The research was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China(No. 2023YFC2411903).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiangdong Cui, a graduatestudent, mainly researches lithium-ion battery energy management systems and lithiumion battery state of charge estimation. 23210720147@m.fudan.edu.cn
Huang Yanqi, an engineer, mainly researches cardiac electrophysiological simulation, medical electronic system design, medical signal processing, etc. yqhuang@fudan.edu.cn
Wu Xiaomei (corresponding author), professor, mainly researches medical electronics, cardiac electrophysiological diagnosis/treatment methods and systems. xiaomeiwu@fudan.edu.cn
